metadata.rationale: The article is built on a real market setup—steady February core CPI did lead traders to watch PCE and GDP next—but it overstates how decisively one CPI print established a macro turning point and pairs that framing with an edited event-card image that misleadingly recasts separate source material as direct visual proof of the CPI story.

prediction.predicted_label: verdict=Mostly False | taxonomy=T1 Fabrication; V1 Synthetic Visual Content; V2 Visual Editing; C2 Contextual Inconsistency

best_trajectory.thought: First, I need to verify the standard reporting practices of the Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) regarding CPI release times to see if the text's claim of a Wednesday release at 8:30 AM ET aligns with the standard schedule, or if it's an exception or a distortion. Checking Wikipedia will establish the baseline for these release schedules.
best_trajectory.action: Wikipedia[US Consumer Price Index release schedule]
best_trajectory.observation: Nasdaq-100 (NDX) is a stock market index made up of equity securities issued by 100 of the largest non-financial companies listed on the Nasdaq stock exchange. It is a modified capitalization-weighted index. The stocks' weights in the index are based on their market capitalizations, with certain rules capping the influence of the largest components. It is limited to companies from a single exchange, and it does not have any financial companies. The financial companies are in a separate index, the Nasdaq Financial-100.
